title: Wearables-Driven Freeform Handwriting Authentication
venue: IEEE Transactions on Biometrics, Behavior, and Identity Science

method.input_representation: linear acceleration measurements across three axis (x, y, and z)
method.training_strategy: Penalizing the dominant class and data augmentation (scaling and rotation)

evaluation.context: Achieved on Scenario E1 (copying same speech) using DNN with 49-window majority voting
Average population FAR for DNN after trained impersonation attacks

evaluation_protocol: Cross-day testing: training/validation on day 1, testing on day 2. Majority voting used to fuse decisions across multiple 5-second windows.

reported_limitations: EER is above acceptable levels for a standalone unimodal system
Higher cognitive load of freeform writing compared to signatures causes more variance

key_contribution: First study of wearable-driven freeform handwriting authentication
Comprehensive evaluation against trained impersonators using visual and video cues
Comparison between manual feature engineering and deep learning (CNN-BLSTM)
main_strength: Rigorous threat model with motivated, trained attackers
Cross-day validation to account for behavioral variance
main_weakness: Performance not sufficient for unimodal high-security applications
Dataset collected in a seated lab environment
insights.notes_for_review: The authors suggest using this as part of a multimodal system due to the EER being around 5-7%.
